title: Throwing a set of matching espresso cups
subtitle: Repeating a small cylinder to a consistent size at the wheel
excerpt: A set of four wheel-thrown stoneware espresso cups produced to a matched height and diameter by measuring each piece as it is thrown. Making a matched set requires measuring the height and rim diameter of the first cup and reproducing that measurement in each subsequent cup using calipers.
sourceNotes: Matched-set throwing technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: Compressing and aligning clay on the spinning wheel head until it runs perfectly true.
Sprung-arm measuring tool for transferring an inside diameter (such as a cup rim) to compare across multiple pieces.
A clay drying stage where the piece is firm enough to trim without distorting but still slightly damp.
A trimmed ring on the underside of a thrown vessel that lifts the base and gives it a refined finish.

content.text: Throwing a matched set requires that each cup be thrown with the same clay weight, opened to the same floor depth, and pulled to the same height and rim diameter. The first cup sets the target. Use

to record the rim diameter of the first cup, then match each subsequent cup against that measurement before cutting off. Variation across a hand-made set of four is acceptable at 3-5 mm; more than that will be visible on the table.

attrs.content: Wear an N95 mask when trimming leather-hard stoneware. Trimming generates fine silica-bearing dust. Fire kilns in a well-ventilated space.

content.text: Weigh four balls of clay to exactly the same weight: 250 g is a good starting point for a 90 ml espresso cup. Uniform clay weight is the first step toward a uniform set. Without it, every throwing decision leads to more variation.

the first ball and throw a straight-sided cup 70 mm tall and 55 mm in rim diameter. Use the needle tool to mark the target height by pressing it lightly into the outer wall at 70 mm above the bat. Set the calipers to the rim diameter of this first cup and record both measurements. Leave this cup on its bat as the visual reference.

For each subsequent cup, open and pull to the needle-marked height. Before cutting off, hold the calipers across the rim and adjust the diameter to match. The technique is to throw slightly taller and narrower than the target, then ease the rim outward to the caliper setting at the end of the session. Cut each cup off cleanly and move it to a drying board.
, invert each one onto the wheel head, centre, and trim a
with the loop tool. Keep the foot ring dimensions consistent across all four cups. Use the calipers to check the foot ring diameter of the first trimmed cup and match subsequent ones to it.
Add a pulled handle to each cup at the leather-hard stage for a full demitasse set.
Use the same method to throw a set of mugs, doubling the clay weight and target height.

items.symptom: Cups vary noticeably in height despite the same clay weight
items.cause: Floor thickness inconsistent; thick-floored cups end up shorter
items.fix: Check floor depth by pressing the needle tool through the inside base until it just stops. Each cup should show the same depth mark

items.symptom: Rim diameters difficult to match accurately
items.cause: Clay spring-back varies between pieces depending on how hard the final pull was
items.fix: Always check the caliper measurement after the wheel stops, not while spinning. The clay relaxes slightly as it slows
